---
title: "Who Gets the Good Rates?"
subtitle: "FICO, LTV, and Credit Quality Across the ABS-EE Universe"
description: "A first look at borrower credit quality across 5.4 million active securitized auto loans — how FICO and LTV vary by deal sponsor, vehicle make, and credit tier."
date: 2026-06-06
categories: [structured-finance, consumer-credit, abs-ee, credit-quality]
jupyter: python3
freeze: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{python}
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

PERIOD = "2026-04"
OUTPUTS = Path("../../analysis/outputs")

by_deal = pd.read_parquet(OUTPUTS / f"fico_by_deal_{PERIOD}.parquet")
by_make = pd.read_parquet(OUTPUTS / f"fico_by_make_{PERIOD}.parquet")
by_tier = pd.read_parquet(OUTPUTS / f"fico_by_tier_{PERIOD}.parquet")

# Tier totals (collapse new/used)
tier_totals = (
    by_tier.groupby("tier")[["loan_count", "orig_bal_mm", "avg_ltv"]]
    .agg({"loan_count": "sum", "orig_bal_mm": "sum", "avg_ltv": "mean"})
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values("tier")
)

# Top 20 deals by loan count
top_deals = by_deal.nlargest(20, "loan_count").copy()

# Top 20 makes by loan count
top_makes = (
    by_make.groupby("make")
    .agg(loan_count=("loan_count", "sum"),
         median_fico=("median_fico", "mean"),
         avg_ltv=("avg_ltv", "mean"))
    .reset_index()
    .nlargest(20, "loan_count")
    .sort_values("median_fico")
)

ojs_define(
    tier_totals=tier_totals.to_dict(orient="records"),
    top_deals=top_deals.to_dict(orient="records"),
    top_makes=top_makes.to_dict(orient="records"),
    period=PERIOD,
)
```

Every month, auto loan securitization trusts file loan-level data with the SEC under
Form ABS-EE: balance, payment status, days past due, vehicle make, borrower credit
score — for every individual loan in the pool. It's public. It's on EDGAR. Most
people don't parse it.

In April 2026 the filed universe covers **5.4 million active loans** across 171 trusts.
This note looks at one slice: who's borrowing, what their credit profile looked like
at origination, and how much the car was worth relative to the loan.

- **FICO** — obligor credit score at origination (when the lender was still optimistic)
- **LTV** — original loan amount ÷ vehicle value at origination
- **Credit tier** — FICO buckets: prime+, prime, near-prime, subprime, deep sub

---

## Credit tier mix

About a third of the filed pool is prime-plus (FICO 750+). The deep subprime shelf —
below 620 — is nearly as large. This is what a cross-section of the US securitized
auto market looks like. It's not a subprime story, and it's not a prime story. It's
both, in the same dataset, filed to the same regulator, available for free.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const TIER_COLOR = {
    "750+  Prime+":        "#4A6FA5",
    "700-749  Prime":      "#5A8A8A",
    "660-699  Near-Prime": "#7EA870",
    "620-659  Subprime":   "#C8964A",
    "<620  Deep Sub":      "#C86A50",
  }

  const tierOrder = [
    "<620  Deep Sub",
    "620-659  Subprime",
    "660-699  Near-Prime",
    "700-749  Prime",
    "750+  Prime+",
  ]

  const countChart = Plot.plot({
    title: "By loan count",
    marginLeft: 140,
    x: { label: "Loans (millions)", tickFormat: d => `${(d/1e6).toFixed(1)}M` },
    y: { domain: tierOrder, label: null },
    style: { background: "transparent", fontSize: "12px" },
    marks: [
      Plot.barX(tier_totals, {
        x: "loan_count",
        y: "tier",
        fill: d => TIER_COLOR[d.tier] ?? "#8A8A8A",
        rx: 2,
      }),
      Plot.ruleX([0]),
    ]
  })

  const balChart = Plot.plot({
    title: "By original balance",
    marginLeft: 140,
    x: { label: "Original balance ($B)", tickFormat: d => `$${(d/1000).toFixed(0)}B` },
    y: { domain: tierOrder, label: null },
    style: { background: "transparent", fontSize: "12px" },
    marks: [
      Plot.barX(tier_totals, {
        x: "orig_bal_mm",
        y: "tier",
        fill: d => TIER_COLOR[d.tier] ?? "#8A8A8A",
        rx: 2,
      }),
      Plot.ruleX([0]),
    ]
  })

  return html`<div style="display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:2rem;margin:1.5rem 0">
    ${countChart}${balChart}
  </div>
  <div class="chart-caption">Source: SEC EDGAR Form ABS-EE — autoloan asset class. As of ${period} reporting period.</div>`
}
```

---

## When the loan is bigger than the car

Prime borrowers borrow about 90 cents for every dollar of vehicle value. Deep subprime
borrowers borrow $1.16 for every dollar — on day one, before the car has left the lot.

That gap matters when things go wrong. Repossess a car from a prime borrower and the
proceeds likely cover the balance. Repossess one from a deep subprime borrower and
you're eating a loss before the first payment was ever missed.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const TIER_COLOR = {
    "750+  Prime+":        "#4A6FA5",
    "700-749  Prime":      "#5A8A8A",
    "660-699  Near-Prime": "#7EA870",
    "620-659  Subprime":   "#C8964A",
    "<620  Deep Sub":      "#C86A50",
  }

  const tierOrder = [
    "<620  Deep Sub",
    "620-659  Subprime",
    "660-699  Near-Prime",
    "700-749  Prime",
    "750+  Prime+",
  ]

  const chart = Plot.plot({
    marginLeft: 140,
    x: {
      label: "Average LTV at origination",
      domain: [0.75, 1.65],
      tickFormat: d => d.toFixed(2),
    },
    y: { domain: tierOrder, label: null },
    style: { background: "transparent", fontSize: "12px" },
    marks: [
      Plot.barX(tier_totals, {
        x: "avg_ltv",
        y: "tier",
        fill: d => TIER_COLOR[d.tier] ?? "#8A8A8A",
        rx: 2,
      }),
      Plot.ruleX([1.0], { stroke: "#8A8A8A", strokeDasharray: "4,3", strokeWidth: 1.5 }),
      Plot.text(tier_totals, {
        x: "avg_ltv",
        y: "tier",
        text: d => d.avg_ltv.toFixed(2),
        dx: 6,
        fontSize: 11,
        fill: "currentColor",
      }),
      Plot.ruleX([0]),
    ]
  })

  return html`<div style="margin:1.5rem 0">
    ${chart}
    <div style="margin-top:0.5rem;font-size:0.72rem;color:#8A8A8A;font-family:'DM Mono',monospace">
      Dashed line = LTV 1.0 (loan equals vehicle value)
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="chart-caption">Source: SEC EDGAR Form ABS-EE — autoloan asset class. As of ${period} reporting period.</div>`
}
```

---

## The primest shelves in the room

Captive finance arms lend to the customers buying their own cars. GM Financial
(median FICO 786, avg LTV 0.93), Nissan (797, 0.90), BMW (801, 0.83) — employed people
with decent credit, buying a car they want, at a dealer motivated to close the sale.
Good loans, almost by construction.

Bridgecrest (FICO 567, LTV 1.53) is a different business. The average Bridgecrest loan
at origination is worth 53% more than the underlying vehicle. That's not a mistake —
it's how subprime auto securitization works. The yield has to compensate for something.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const maxLoans = Math.max(...top_deals.map(d => d.loan_count))

  const chart = Plot.plot({
    marginLeft: 20,
    marginRight: 20,
    width: 700,
    height: 380,
    x: {
      label: "Median FICO",
      domain: [530, 830],
      grid: true,
      tickSpacing: 50,
    },
    y: {
      label: "Average LTV",
      domain: [0.7, 1.7],
      tickFormat: d => d.toFixed(2),
    },
    color: {
      scheme: "RdYlGn",
      domain: [540, 820],
      legend: true,
      label: "Median FICO",
    },
    style: { background: "transparent", fontSize: "12px" },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([1.0], { stroke: "#8A8A8A", strokeDasharray: "4,3", strokeWidth: 1 }),
      Plot.dot(top_deals, {
        x: "median_fico",
        y: "avg_ltv",
        r: d => 4 + (d.loan_count / maxLoans) * 18,
        fill: "median_fico",
        fillOpacity: 0.82,
        stroke: "currentColor",
        strokeWidth: 0.5,
        strokeOpacity: 0.3,
      }),
      Plot.text(top_deals, {
        x: "median_fico",
        y: "avg_ltv",
        text: d => (d.sponsor ?? "").slice(0, 18),
        fontSize: 9,
        dx: 2,
        dy: -10,
        fill: "currentColor",
      }),
    ]
  })

  return html`<div style="margin:1.5rem 0">${chart}</div>
  <div class="chart-caption">Top 20 deals by loan count. Bubble size = loan count.
  Source: SEC EDGAR Form ABS-EE. As of ${period} reporting period.</div>`
}
```

---

## The car doesn't know who's driving it

The same Toyota Camry gets financed by a FICO-780 Capital One Prime customer and a
FICO-580 Exeter customer. Luxury brands cluster at the top — the overlap between
"people who buy new BMWs" and "people with 800 FICOs" is real, and not particularly
surprising.

The interesting cases are volume brands with wide distributions: same make, very
different borrower. At that point the brand tells you almost nothing about credit
quality — what matters is who underwrote it.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const ficoMin = Math.min(...top_makes.map(d => d.median_fico))
  const ficoMax = Math.max(...top_makes.map(d => d.median_fico))

  const chart = Plot.plot({
    marginLeft: 100,
    x: {
      label: "Median FICO at origination",
      domain: [560, 820],
    },
    y: {
      domain: top_makes.map(d => d.make),
      label: null,
    },
    color: {
      scheme: "RdYlGn",
      domain: [ficoMin, ficoMax],
    },
    style: { background: "transparent", fontSize: "12px" },
    marks: [
      Plot.barX(top_makes, {
        x: "median_fico",
        y: "make",
        fill: "median_fico",
        rx: 2,
        sort: { y: "x" },
      }),
      Plot.text(top_makes, {
        x: "median_fico",
        y: "make",
        text: d => d.median_fico.toFixed(0),
        dx: 6,
        fontSize: 10,
        fill: "currentColor",
        sort: { y: "x" },
      }),
      Plot.ruleX([560]),
    ]
  })

  return html`<div style="margin:1.5rem 0">${chart}</div>
  <div class="chart-caption">Top 20 vehicle makes by loan count. Median FICO across all trusts reporting that make.
  Source: SEC EDGAR Form ABS-EE. As of ${period} reporting period.</div>`
}
```

---

## Notes on the data

- **FICO missing rate**: ~3.5% of loans in this period have no credit score on file —
  excluded from all charts above. The SEC doesn't require FICO reporting under ABS-EE;
  trusts report it voluntarily.
- **Schema variation**: Different trusts file slightly different field sets. Handled
  with a `union_by_name` merge; fields a given trust doesn't report appear as null.
- **FICO timing**: The score is from origination — when the lender was still optimistic.
  Loans two years into the pool may look quite different today.
- **Source**: SEC EDGAR Form ABS-EE, Schedule AL — autoloan asset class.
  Reporting period ending 2026-03-31, filed April 2026.
